Hydrated Ions (1 of 2)
Ions and molecules in solution are surrounded by an organized sheath of solvent molecules.
Smaller, more highly charged ions
Bind more H 2 O molecules
Behave as larger species in solution
Estimated Number of Waters of Hydration

Hydrated Ions (2 of 2)
Water binds to cations through the oxygen bonds.

The Effect of Ionic Strength on Solubility of Salts: Effect of Ionic Strength on Ionic Dissociation
Figure 8-1
Consider the following equilibrium reaction:
The equilibrium “constant” is not really constant
Color Plate 4

Why does the solubility increase when salts are added to the solution?
Ionic atmosphere
Region of solution around an ion or charged particle
Contains an excess of the oppositely charged ions
The greater the ionic strength of solution , the higher the charge on each ionic atmosphere.
Cation and anion attraction is reduced (relative to attraction in distilled water)
Tendency of ions to come together is reduced
Solubility of salt is increased
Figure 8-2
Increasing Ionic Strength Promotes Dissociation

Effect of Added Salts on Solubility
Compare the effect on potassium hydrogen tartrate solubility when each compound is added to solution.
KCl
NaCl
MgSO 4
Glucose

Ionic Strength
Ionic strength (  ) is a measure of the total concentration of ions in solution.
Example: Find the ionic strength of (a) 0.10 M NaNO 3 ; (b) 0.010 M Na 2 SO 4 ; and (c) 0.020 M KBr plus 0.010 M Na 2 SO 4 (d) 1 mM CaCl 2

Example: Calculation of Ionic Strength (1 of 3)
Solution
Note that [Na + ] = 0.020 M because there are two moles of Na + per mole of Na 2 SO 4 .

Example: Calculation of Ionic Strength (3 of 4)
Solution

Charge and Stoichiometry Affect Ionic Strength
Electrolyte | Molarity | Ionic strength
1: 1 | M | M
2: 1 | M | 3M
3: 1 | M | 6M
2: 2 | M | 4M

Activity Coefficients To account for the effect of ionic strength, concentrations are replaced by activities
Activity (
)
Value that replaces concentration in a thermodynamically correct equilibrium expression
Activity coefficient (  )
Measures the deviation of behavior from ideality
Numeric value used to adjust concentration for ionic strength
If  = 1, behavior would be ideal

Reaction:
The correct form of the equilibrium constant, K ,  accounts for ionic strength. ( Equation 8-5)

Consider the Reaction (1 of 2)
From Equation 8-5:
At low ionic strength ( μ → 0 M),  approaches unity.
If  = 1, behavior would be ideal.
K approaches the concentration quotient.
Increase ionic strength (↑ μ ) by adding a second salt.
[Ca 2+ ] and
will increase.
Activity coefficients (  ) must decrease.

Example: Exponents of Activity Coefficients (1 of 3)
Write the solubility product expression for
with activity coefficients.
Why are chemical activities different from chemical concentrations?

Determining Activity Coefficients
Extended Debye- Hückel equation
 = activity coefficient
 = ionic strength
z = ion charge
 = ion size (in picometers, pm)
At moderate ionic strengths
(0 M     0.1 M), Debye- Hückel equation works well.
At high ionic strengths
( 0.1 M    2-6 mol /kg), the more complicated Pitzer equations are used.

Table 8-1 Activity Coefficients for Aqueous Solutions at 25°C (1 of 4)
All ions of the same size and charge appear in the same group and have the same .

Table 8-1 Activity Coefficients for Aqueous Solutions at 25°C (2 of 4)
Note:  Ion sizes are empirical parameters.
Sizes cannot be taken literally. However, trends are sensible.
Small highly charged ions bind solvent more tightly and have larger effective radius.
Table 8-1 Activity Coefficients for Aqueous Solutions at 25°C (3 of 4)
Table 8-1 Activity Coefficients for Aqueous Solutions at 25°C (4 of 4)

As Ionic Strength Increases from 0 to 0.1 M
As ionic strength increases , activity coefficient decreases .
Activity coefficient (  ) approaches unity as ionic strength (  ) approaches 0.
As magnitude of the charge ( z ) of the ion increases, activity coefficient (  ) deviates from unity.
Activity corrections are more important for ions with charge of  3 than charge of  1.
The smaller the ion size (  ), the more important activity effects become.
Figure 8-4

Find the activity coefficient of Ca 2+ in a solution of 3.3 mM CaCl 2 .

Example: Using Table 8-1 (2 of 3)
Solution
The ionic strength is
In Table 8-1, Ca 2+ is listed under the charge ±2 and has a size of 600 pm. Thus  = 0.675 when μ = 0.010 M.

Example: Using Table 8-1 (3 of 3)
Test Yourself: Find 𝛾 for Cl − in 0.33 mM CaCl 2 .
[Speaker Notes] Answer: 0.964

Interpolating Activity Coefficients
Linear interpolation
Assume the values between two neighboring entries of a table can be approximated by a straight line.
Fast alternative to the Debye- Hückel equation when calculating activity coefficients.
Do not confuse two terms:
Interpolation estimates a number between two values on a table.
Extrapolation estimates a number that lies beyond the values on a table.

Interpolating Activity Coefficients Using Table 8.1
Linear interpolation is a simple ratio:
x = 16 is 60% of the way from 10 to 20.
Therefore, the corresponding y -value will be 60% of the way from 0.67 to 0.83.

Example: Interpolating Activity Coefficients (1 of 4)
Calculate the activity coefficient of H + when μ = 0.025 M.

Example: Interpolating Activity Coefficients (2 of 4)
Solution
H + is the first entry in Table 8-1.
The linear interpolation is set up as follows:

Activity Coefficients of Nonionic Compounds
Neutral molecules have an activity coefficient   1  (when  < 0.1 M).
Activity  concentration for neutral species
Gases have an activity coefficient   1  (when pressure < 1 bar).
Activity  pressure of the gas
Note: fugacity = the activity of a gas
Deviation from ideal gas behavior is fugacity coefficient

Activity Coefficients at High Ionic Strength
At high ionic strengths,   1 M.
Activity coefficients deviate from trends observed on Table 8-1.
 increases with increasing .
Figure 8-5 :
in NaClO 4 solutions of high ionic strength.
Behavior differs from dilute aqueous solutions.
Solvent is no longer pure H 2 O, but a mixture of H 2 O and salt.
Figure 8-5
